rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF JAMES WON LING BONG
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Requests For Admission, Set No. Four, And For Monetary Sanctions Against Respondent Annie Tang, Individually, And Her Attorney
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 2] Tentative ruling by Judge Ross C. Moody. Before the Court are Petitioner Mary Bong's two related discovery motions: (1) Motion to Compel Further Responses to Requests for Admission, and (2) Motion to Compel Further Responses to Form Interrogatory No. 17.1 Relating to Requests for Admission. Both motions are GRANTED. Petitioner seeks to compel further responses to nine requests for admission and twelve requests for genuineness of documents. The Requests for Admission relate to a matter in controversy, namely, whether the approximately $7.5 million balance owed on loans by Decedent's business, James Bong Enterprise Inc., to Decedent was transferred to the Fortune 8 Trust, or is an asset of the probate estate. The Requests for Genuineness of Documents relate to the Corporate Tax Returns of James Bong Enterprise Inc., which show the monies loaned by Decedent to his business and the interest payments made on these loans which went to Decedent until his death. To each of the nine Requests for Admission, Respondent objected on the grounds of standing and relevance. Subject to the foregoing objections, Respondent further stated that she lacked information sufficient to admit or deny the requests following a reasonable inquiry. On reply in support of the motion, Petitioner attached email communications produced in discovery between Respondent's counsel, Mr. Hafter, and Decedent's estate planning attorney, Mr. Way Pung. In an email dated May 15, 2023, Mr. Hafter enlists Mr. Pung's assistance in confirming that Respondent's "Admit" responses to a series of requests for admission were in fact, correct. Mr. Hafter writes: "We are responding to some discovery requests in the Bong Estate case. Betty suggested that I confirm that the following responses are correct." Mr. Pung, in response, noted "Confirmed" in red ink after each request for admission. Mr. Hafter acknowledged Mr. Pung's email on May 18, 2023 with a "Thank you." Despite having confirmed that the "Admit" responses were correct, Respondent served Petitioner objections, and stated she "lack[ed] information or knowledge sufficient to admit or deny th[e] request[s]." In a Declaration filed in response to Petitioner's reply, Mr. Hafter claims that after receiving Mr. Pung's responses, he questioned whether Mr. Pung actually possessed the knowledge to know if any sums at issue were disbursed following 2014, and after conferring with Respondent, concluded that Respondent lacked knowledge, following a reasonable inquiry, to make the requested admissions. Mr. Hafter's explanation is not persuasive. A party claiming inability to admit or deny a request for admission must investigate sources reasonably available to it in formulating its responses to requests for admission and state that a reasonable inquiry was made to obtain sufficient information. (Code Civ. Proc, sec. 2033.220(c); Chodos v. Superior Court (1963) 215 Cal.App.2d 318, 322, overruled on other grounds.) As explained more fully below, it is simply not credible for Respondent to claim that she and her counsel conducted a reasonable investigation and made a good faith effort to obtain the information needed to properly respond to the discovery but were unable to learn enough to admit or deny. Request Nos. 185-187 ask about specific financial dealings between Fortune 8 Trust and James Bong Enterprise Inc. Request Nos. 190-192 and Nos. 194-196 ask about specific financial dealings between Fortune 8 Trust and Decedent. Respondent is and has been the Chief Financial Officer of James Bong Enterprise Inc. since 2019 and the Chief Executive Officer since 2022, and has admitted that she is the Trustee of Fortune 8 Trust. As such, she has access to the books and records of both James Bong Enterprise Inc. and Fortune 8 Trust, as well as to the banking records for those entities. [Part 1 of 2]

rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Form Interrogatory No. 17.1 Relating To Requests For Admissions, Set No. Four, And For Monetary Sanctions Against Respondent Annie Tang, Individually, And Her Attorney
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 2] Tentative ruling by Judge Ross C. Moody. Before the Court are Petitioner Mary Bong's two related discovery motions: (1) Motion to Compel Further Responses to Requests for Admission, and (2) Motion to Compel Further Responses to Form Interrogatory No. 17.1 Relating to Requests for Admission. Both motions are GRANTED. Petitioner seeks to compel further responses to nine requests for admission and twelve requests for genuineness of documents. The Requests for Admission relate to a matter in controversy, namely, whether the approximately $7.5 million balance owed on loans by Decedent's business, James Bong Enterprise Inc., to Decedent was transferred to the Fortune 8 Trust, or is an asset of the probate estate. Mr. Hafter writes: "We are responding to some discovery requests in the Bong Estate case. Betty suggested that I confirm that the following responses are correct." rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF JAMES WON LING BONG
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion To Compel Further Responses To Requests For Admission, Set No. Four, And For Monetary Sanctions Against Respondent Annie Tang, Individually, And Her Attorney (Added For Posting Of The Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 2] The email exchange between Respondent's counsel and Mr. Pung tends to establish counsel had developed sufficient information from his investigation for Respondent to admit all the RFAs. Respondent's counsel did not ask Mr. Pung whether the requests should be admitted or denied; he shared with Mr. Pung his conclusion that they should be admitted, and asked for confirmation of that conclusion. Put another way, Respondent (or at least her counsel) apparently used Mr. Pung as part of the investigation into the RFAs and sought confirmation from Mr. Pung of what Respondent and her counsel had already concluded, which he confirmed. Request for Admission Exhibits 22-33 relate to the genuineness of James Bong Enterprise Inc.'s Corporate Tax Returns from 2010-2021. They were obtained via subpoena from ASAM, LLP, accountants for James Bong Enterprise Inc. These very same tax returns were also produced by James Bong Enterprise Inc. in response to a subpoena, albeit with redactions. Given Respondent's position within the corporation and her access to the tax returns, the Court agrees with Petitioner that Respondent's position with regards to not being able to authenticate these documents is untenable. In light of the email evidence and Respondent's status as CEO and CFO of James Bong Enterprise Inc. and Trustee of Fortune 8 Trust, the Court cannot credit Respondent's claim that she and her counsel conducted a reasonable investigation and made a good faith effort to obtain the information but were unable to learn sufficient facts to either confirm or deny the requests. Under the circumstances, it is neither unfair nor improper to order Respondent to properly respond to Request for Admission Nos. 185-187, 190-192, 194-196, Form Interrogatory 17.1, and the authentication of documents. Respondent is ordered to provide further responses within ten (10) days of entry of this order. Responses to the Requests for Admission shall be either admit or deny, and if denied, Respondent must provide responses to Form Interrogatory 17.1. The Court finds that Respondent acted without sufficient justification in claiming that she could not respond to the discovery propounded following a reasonable investigation, and in opposing this motion to compel. The Court further finds that it would not be unjust to impose sanctions for this conduct. Good cause having been shown, sanctions in the amount of $4,680.55 is awarded to Petitioner, and shall be assessed against Respondent and her counsel. Respondent's request for sanctions is denied. Counsel for Petitioner shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling as published above. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204. [Part 2 of 2]
